questions.question: Explain the three-way handshake process in TCP connection establishment.
keyPoints: Client sends SYN segment with sequence number x
Server responds with SYN-ACK containing sequence number y and acknowledgment x+1
Client sends ACK with acknowledgment y+1, connection established

questions.question: List and explain three differences between TCP and UDP.
keyPoints: TCP is connection-oriented; UDP is connectionless
TCP provides reliability (acknowledgments, retransmissions); UDP does not
TCP ensures ordered delivery; UDP does not guarantee order
TCP has flow control and congestion control; UDP does not

questions.question: What is flow control in TCP? How does it work?
keyPoints: Flow control prevents sender from overwhelming receiver
Uses receive window field in TCP header
Receiver advertises available buffer space
Sender adjusts transmission rate accordingly

questions.question: Explain the four-way handshake process for TCP connection termination.
keyPoints: Client sends FIN with sequence number
Server sends ACK acknowledging the FIN
Server sends FIN when done sending data
Client sends ACK, connection closes after TIME_WAIT

questions.question: What are the different TCP connection states? Explain any three.
keyPoints: LISTEN: Server waiting for connection
SYN_SENT: Client waiting for SYN-ACK
SYN_RCVD: Server waiting for final ACK
ESTABLISHED: Connection active
TIME_WAIT: Client waiting after final ACK

questions.question: Explain the purpose of sequence numbers in TCP.
keyPoints: Track order of data bytes transmitted
Detect missing or duplicate segments
Enable reliable delivery through acknowledgments
Allow proper reassembly of data at receiver

questions.question: What is a TCP socket? Explain active and passive socket.
keyPoints: Socket = IP address + port number
Uniquely identifies one end of TCP connection
Active socket: Client initiates connection (active open)
Passive socket: Server prepares to accept connections (passive open, LISTEN state)

questions.question: Draw and explain the state transition diagram for TCP connection establishment and termination. Include all major states and transitions.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: CLOSED -> LISTEN -> SYN_RCVD -> ESTABLISHED
Three-way handshake transitions with SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
ESTABLISHED -> FIN_WAIT_1 -> FIN_WAIT_2 -> TIME_WAIT -> CLOSED
Server side: CLOSE_WAIT -> LAST_ACK -> CLOSED
Explanation of each state and transition trigger
Purpose of TIME_WAIT state

questions.question: Explain the TCP header format with all fields and their purposes.
keyPoints: Source Port (16 bits)
Destination Port (16 bits)
Sequence Number (32 bits)
Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)
Data Offset, Reserved, Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN)
Window Size (16 bits)
Checksum (16 bits)
Urgent Pointer (16 bits)
Options and Padding (variable)
Data section

questions.question: Explain in detail how TCP ensures reliable data transfer.
keyPoints: Use of sequence numbers for ordering
Acknowledgments (ACKs) for received data
Retransmission timeout mechanism
Checksum for error detection
Flow control to prevent overflow
Cumulative acknowledgments
Handling duplicate segments
Sliding window protocol

questions.question: Compare TCP and UDP in detail, explaining when each protocol should be used.
keyPoints: TCP: Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, flow control, congestion control
UDP: Connectionless, unreliable, no ordering, no flow control
TCP overhead vs UDP simplicity
Use TCP for: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SSH, databases
Use UDP for: DNS, DHCP, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming
Trade-offs between reliability and latency
